questions.question: Define covariance and state its formula. Explain what a positive, negative, and zero covariance indicates about the relationship between two variables.
keyPoints: Covariance measures joint variability: Cov(X,Y) = E[(X-μx)(Y-μy)]
Positive covariance: variables increase or decrease together
Negative covariance: one increases while the other decreases
Zero covariance: variables may be independent or have non-linear relationship

questions.question: What is Pearson's correlation coefficient? Explain why it is preferred over covariance for comparing relationships between different variable pairs.
keyPoints: Pearson r = Cov(X,Y) / (σx × σy)
Range: -1 ≤ r ≤ 1
Unitless (scale-independent)
Allows fair comparison between different pairs of variables
Easier to interpret due to bounded range

questions.question: State and explain any four properties of the correlation coefficient.
keyPoints: Symmetric: r(xy) = r(yx)
Bounded: -1 ≤ r ≤ 1
Unitless: not affected by scale of measurement
Unaffected by linear transformations
r = ±1 implies perfect linear relationship
r = 0 indicates no linear correlation

questions.question: Differentiate between independent variables and uncorrelated variables with examples.
keyPoints: Independent: one variable does not affect another statistically
Uncorrelated (r=0): no linear relationship exists
Independence implies zero correlation but not vice versa
Example: If Y = X² with X taking negative and positive values, r ≈ 0 but they are dependent

questions.question: What are the limitations of correlation coefficient as a measure of relationship between variables?
keyPoints: Only measures linear relationships, misses non-linear associations
Sensitive to outliers which can distort results
Does not imply causation
Can be misleading with small sample sizes
Zero correlation does not mean independence (can have non-linear relationship)

questions.question: Calculate the covariance and correlation coefficient for the following paired data: X: 2, 4, 6, 8, 10; Y: 1, 3, 5, 7, 9
keyPoints: Cov(X,Y) = Σ(xi-x̄)(yi-ȳ)/(n-1) = 10
r = Cov/(sx×sy) = 10/10 = 1 (perfect positive correlation)

questions.question: Two variables X and Y have Cov(X,Y) = 25, Var(X) = 16, and Var(Y) = 9. Find the correlation coefficient between X and Y.
keyPoints: r = Cov(X,Y) / (σx × σy)
Note: This is impossible - correlation cannot exceed 1, indicating data error or outlier effect

questions.question: Explain the relationship between covariance and correlation coefficient. Derive the formula showing how correlation is obtained from covariance. Also explain why two variables with zero correlation may not be independent.
keyPoints: Correlation = Covariance / (σx × σy)
Covariance can be any real number, correlation is always in [-1,1]
Covariance affected by scale, correlation is unitless
Zero correlation means no LINEAR relationship but non-linear relationship may exist
Example: Y = X² with X ~ N(0,1) has r ≈ 0 but variables are clearly dependent
Independence requires ALL relationships to be absent, zero correlation only checks linear relationship

questions.question: What is the difference between positive, negative, and zero correlation? With the help of scatter diagrams, explain the interpretation of correlation coefficient values of +1, -1, and 0.
keyPoints: Positive: points trend upward, r > 0
Negative: points trend downward, r < 0
Zero: no linear pattern, random scatter
r = +1: all points on upward straight line (perfect positive)
r = -1: all points on downward straight line (perfect negative)
r = 0: no linear pattern, could be circular or random

questions.question: Discuss the assumptions and conditions for using Pearson's correlation coefficient. What precautions should be taken when interpreting correlation results?
keyPoints: Assumptions: linear relationship, continuous variables, bivariate normality, no significant outliers
Precautions: correlation ≠ causation
Check for outliers before interpreting
Use large sample sizes for reliable results
Consider non-linear relationships when r ≈ 0
Report both correlation and sample size

questions.question: Given the following bivariate data, calculate Pearson's correlation coefficient manually showing all steps: X: 10, 12, 15, 18, 20; Y: 25, 28, 32, 35, 40
keyPoints: Calculate means: x̄ = 15, Ȳ = 32
Compute deviations and products: Σ(x-x̄)(y-ȳ) = 115
Compute squared deviations: Σ(x-x̄)² = 68, Σ(y-ȳ)² = 146
Correct calculation yields r ≈ 0.994 (strong positive correlation)
Interpretation: Very strong positive linear relationship between X and Y
